when asked to verify that a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, the agent must check for all active DVT prophylaxis orders and, if multiple are found, actually discontinue all but the newest order, ensuring only the most recent remains active—the main mistake is failing to actually discontinue duplicate orders rather than just reporting the absence or need for a new order.

when asked to check if a urinary catheter has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, the agent must confirm that there are no active, completed, or prior removal orders or service requests for catheter removal; if any removal order or service request (even if completed or pending) exists, the agent should NOT create a new service request.

when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order for a patient has a matching naloxone prescription, the agent must explicitly check for all active opioid orders and, for each one found, confirm the presence of a corresponding naloxone prescription; if any opioid order lacks a matching naloxone prescription, the agent should create a new naloxone order for it—the main mistake is failing to ensure this match and incorrectly concluding the task is complete without confirming or creating naloxone orders if active opioid orders exist.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 hours and the past 12 hours, the agent must independently check for heart rate measurements in each period and compute the average for each window separately; the main mistake is assuming that if there are no measurements in the 12-hour window, there are also none in the 6-hour window, instead of evaluating and reporting each window individually.

when asked to determine the date of the last influenza vaccine and order a new vaccine if more than 365 days have passed, the agent must treat "no prior influenza vaccine found" as meeting the criteria for a new order and explicitly state that the absence of any prior vaccine is equivalent to more than 365 days since last administration, thus requiring a new vaccine order.

when asked to verify that a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, the agent must check for all active DVT prophylaxis orders and, if multiple are found, actually discontinue all but the newest order, ensuring only the most recent remains active—the main mistake is failing to actually discontinue duplicate orders rather than just describing or reporting the action.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 hours and the past 12 hours, the agent must independently check for heart rate measurements within each time window and report the average for each period separately; the main mistake is assuming that if there are no measurements in the 12-hour window, there are also none in the 6-hour window, instead of evaluating and reporting each window individually.

when asked to verify that a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, the agent must check for all active DVT prophylaxis orders and, if multiple are found, actually discontinue all but the newest order, ensuring only the most recent remains active—the main mistake is failing to actually discontinue duplicate orders rather than just creating a new order or reporting the count.

when asked to determine the date of the last influenza vaccine and order a new vaccine if more than 365 days have passed, the agent must treat "no prior influenza vaccine found" as meeting the criteria for a new order and explicitly state that the absence of any prior vaccine is equivalent to more than 365 days since last administration, thus requiring a new vaccine order.
